name: Microwave Sickness
description: Microwave sickness, also known as radiofrequency sickness or electromagnetic hypersensitivity (EHS), is a controversial and contested theory that prolonged exposure to electromagnetic fields (EMF), particularly those emitted by microwave ovens, mobile phones, Wi-Fi routers, and other electronic devices, can cause a range of adverse health effects. Proponents claim that symptoms can include headaches, fatigue, dizziness, sleep disturbances, cognitive dysfunction, skin problems, and even more severe conditions. The theory gained traction in the latter half of the 20th century, particularly following reports from Eastern European countries and the Soviet Union regarding workers exposed to radar technology. These reports, often lacking rigorous scientific methodology by modern standards, linked EMF exposure to various health issues. Key evidence cited by proponents includes anecdotal accounts of individuals experiencing symptom relief upon reducing EMF exposure, as well as some studies suggesting biological effects of EMF on cells or animals. However, mainstream science generally dismisses microwave sickness as a distinct medical condition. Double-blind, placebo-controlled studies have consistently failed to demonstrate a causal relationship between EMF exposure and reported symptoms. The prevailing scientific view attributes these symptoms to psychological factors such as the nocebo effect (expecting harm from EMF exposure) or pre-existing medical conditions. Organizations like the World Health Organization (WHO) and national health agencies acknowledge potential concerns about EMF exposure but emphasize that current scientific evidence does not support the existence of a specific 'microwave sickness' syndrome. They recommend precautionary measures to minimize exposure but stress that fear and anxiety surrounding EMF may be contributing factors to reported symptoms.
subtitle: Controversial theory of EMF sensitivity.

claims.claim: EMF exposure causes a specific set of symptoms known as microwave sickness.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal reports of symptom relief when reducing EMF exposure; some studies showing biological effects of EMF on cells/animals.
claims.criticism: Double-blind, placebo-controlled studies consistently fail to demonstrate a causal link; symptoms likely due to psychological factors or pre-existing conditions.

claims.claim: Governments and industries are covering up the dangers of EMF exposure.
claims.evidence: Alleged suppression of research showing negative health effects; lobbying efforts by telecommunications companies.
claims.criticism: Lack of concrete evidence of a deliberate cover-up; stringent safety regulations exist to limit EMF exposure.

claims.claim: Individuals with EHS can accurately detect and differentiate between different types of EMF.
claims.evidence: Self-reported ability to sense EMF fields; some studies suggesting physiological responses to EMF in sensitive individuals.
claims.criticism: Double-blind studies show that individuals with EHS cannot reliably distinguish between real and sham EMF exposure; physiological responses not consistently replicated.

keyFigures.name: Olle Johansson
keyFigures.role: Researcher who has studied EHS and EMF effects.

keyFigures.name: Michael Repacholi
keyFigures.role: Scientist who has researched EMF safety and risk assessment.

mythologyConnections: Possible parallels to folklore about unseen forces causing illness
Connections to fears of technological progress and its impact on health

timeline.event: Initial reports from Eastern European countries linking radar exposure to health problems.
Increased public concern over EMF exposure from mobile phones and Wi-Fi.
Ongoing debate and research regarding EMF safety and the existence of EHS.
